title: Adaptive Retrieval (query-type-routed RAG)
one_liner: An LLM classifies each query as Factual, Analytical, Opinion, or Contextual and routes it to a specialized retrieval strategy with tailored query rewriting, sub-query decomposition, and LLM-based reranking.
problem_solved: A one-size-fits-all retrieval pipeline (single top-k similarity search with fixed parameters) is suboptimal across heterogeneous query types: factual lookups need precise, focused retrieval; analytical questions need broad multi-aspect coverage; opinion questions need deliberately diverse viewpoints; and context-dependent questions need the user's situation folded into the query. Adaptive retrieval dispatches each query to a strategy designed for its intent.
failure_modes: My retriever works fine for simple fact lookups but gives shallow or one-sided results for broad 'explain/compare/analyze' questions
Top-k similarity search returns near-duplicate chunks about a single aspect, so multi-faceted questions get incomplete coverage
Questions about opinions or debates return documents that all take the same side instead of representing multiple viewpoints
Vaguely or conversationally phrased queries retrieve imprecise chunks because the raw query embedding is a poor search key
Answers ignore who is asking - queries that depend on user-specific context are retrieved as if they were generic
Irrelevant chunks slip into the context window because raw vector similarity is the only relevance signal (no reranking)
Tuning retrieval parameters for one query type degrades quality on other query types in a mixed workload
when_to_use: Your query workload is genuinely heterogeneous - a mix of fact lookup, analytical/how-why questions, opinion/debate questions, and personalized questions hitting the same knowledge base (e.g. support portals, research assistants, enterprise Q&A)
You observe that a single retrieval configuration is a compromise: raising k helps analytical queries but pollutes factual ones, and vice versa
You have user context (role, history, preferences) available and want contextual queries reformulated to include it
You can tolerate several extra LLM calls of latency and cost per query in exchange for intent-appropriate retrieval
You want a LangChain-compatible drop-in retriever (the notebook wraps the router in a BaseRetriever subclass) so downstream chains stay unchanged
tradeoffs: Heavy per-query LLM overhead: one classification call, one or more query-rewrite/sub-query/viewpoint calls, then point-wise 1-10 relevance scoring of every candidate document (k*2 docs = 8+ scoring calls) - easily 10+ GPT-4o calls per question, with corresponding latency and cost
Silent misrouting: if the classifier picks the wrong category, the query gets a mismatched strategy with no fallback or confidence check
The four categories (Factual/Analytical/Opinion/Contextual) are hand-crafted; your domain's query taxonomy may differ, and adding categories means writing new strategy classes
As implemented, each strategy builds its own separate FAISS index over the same texts, quadrupling embedding and index-build cost - a production version should share one index
Point-wise LLM relevance scoring is noisy and slow compared to a trained cross-encoder reranker
Do not use when queries are homogeneous (e.g. all factual lookup) - a single tuned retriever plus a reranker is simpler, cheaper, and easier to evaluate
The notebook's Opinion strategy contains a latent bug (calls .split() on a structured-output list of ints), so treat the code as a pattern, not production-ready
Notebook demo corpus is a single sentence, so retrieval-quality claims are not empirically demonstrated
how_it_works: At query time an LLM classifier (GPT-4o with structured output via a Pydantic `categories_options` model) labels the query Factual, Analytical, Opinion, or Contextual. An `AdaptiveRetriever` dict-dispatches to one of four strategy classes, all inheriting from `BaseRetrievalStrategy`, which owns a FAISS vector store built from `CharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=800)` chunks embedded with `OpenAIEmbeddings`. Factual: LLM rewrites the query for precision, similarity-search retrieves 2k candidates, then an LLM scores each doc 1-10 for relevance and the top k are kept. Analytical: LLM generates k sub-queries (structured `SubQueries` output), retrieves per sub-query, then an LLM picks a diverse-and-relevant subset by index (`SelectedIndices`). Opinion: LLM enumerates k distinct viewpoints, retrieves per viewpoint, then an LLM selects the most representative diverse opinions. Contextual: LLM reformulates the query with user_context, retrieves 2k, then ranks with a context-aware 1-10 scoring prompt. The router is wrapped in a `PydanticAdaptiveRetriever(BaseRetriever)` so it plugs into standard LangChain chains, and an `AdaptiveRAG` class feeds the selected docs into a grounded answer prompt ('if you don't know, say you don't know'). Though categorized under retrieval, the technique spans query-transformation (rewriting, sub-query decomposition, contextualization), post-retrieval (LLM reranking and diversity selection), and orchestration (classify-and-route control flow).
key_dependencies: langchain (PromptTemplate, FAISS vectorstore wrapper, CharacterTextSplitter, BaseRetriever, Document)
repo-local helper_functions and evaluation.evalute_rag modules (wildcard-imported boilerplate)
key_code_signals: class QueryClassifier with chain = prompt | llm.with_structured_output(categories_options) returning Factual/Analytical/Opinion/Contextual
FactualRetrievalStrategy: 'Enhance this factual query' rewrite, similarity_search(k*2), per-doc 1-10 scoring via with_structured_output(relevant_score)
AnalyticalRetrievalStrategy: SubQueries structured output -> per-sub-query similarity_search -> SelectedIndices diversity-selection prompt
OpinionRetrievalStrategy: 'Identify {k} distinct viewpoints' -> retrieve per viewpoint -> LLM selects diverse opinions by index
ContextualRetrievalStrategy.retrieve(query, k, user_context): context-aware query reformulation and context-aware ranking prompt
PydanticAdaptiveRetriever(BaseRetriever) wrapper making the router a drop-in LangChain retriever
related_techniques: query_transformations: composes - the Factual/Contextual strategies reuse query rewriting and the Analytical strategy reuses sub-query decomposition from that technique
reranking: alternative/composes - a cross-encoder or LLM reranker delivers the post-retrieval scoring step without the routing layer, or can replace the noisy point-wise 1-10 scoring here
self_rag: alternative - adapts at the whether-and-how-to-use-retrieval level with reflection, rather than routing among fixed retrieval strategies
crag (corrective RAG): alternative - adapts based on evaluated retrieval quality after the fact instead of predicted query type before retrieval
retrieval_with_feedback_loop: sibling - the repo's other 'Iterative and Adaptive' technique, adapting from user feedback over time rather than per-query intent
fusion_retrieval: composes - hybrid BM25+vector search could serve as the backend inside each strategy
hyde: alternative - a different query-side transformation for improving retrieval precision on a single strategy
multi_faceted_filtering: composes - metadata/similarity filtering could replace or augment the LLM diversity-selection step
summary: Adaptive retrieval fixes the problem that a single fixed retrieval configuration is a compromise across query types: precise top-k similarity search suits factual lookups but under-covers analytical questions, returns one-sided results for opinion questions, and ignores user context. A GPT-4o classifier with Pydantic structured output labels each query Factual, Analytical, Opinion, or Contextual, and a router dispatches it to a matching strategy over a FAISS index. The Factual strategy rewrites the query and LLM-scores each candidate 1-10; the Analytical strategy decomposes the query into sub-queries, retrieves per sub-query, and LLM-selects a diverse subset; the Opinion strategy enumerates distinct viewpoints and retrieves per viewpoint; the Contextual strategy folds user context into a reformulated query and context-aware ranking. A LangChain BaseRetriever wrapper makes it a drop-in retriever. Costs are real: roughly 10+ LLM calls per query (classification, rewriting, per-document scoring), silent misrouting risk, and hand-crafted categories that may not match your domain. Prefer it for mixed-intent workloads; skip it for homogeneous factual Q&A where a tuned retriever plus reranker is cheaper.
notes_for_chunking: Well-structured notebook, easy to chunk. Cell 0 is a book advertisement (junk, strip). Cell 1 is a long, high-quality standalone overview in markdown (Overview/Motivation/Key Components/Method Details/Benefits/Conclusion) - it makes an excellent summary chunk by itself. Cell 2 is an SVG image reference (strip or keep as pointer). Cells 3-5 are pip install and git-clone boilerplate (strip). Cell 6 is the import block including wildcard imports from repo-local helper_functions (note: `List` comes from these wildcards, so code cells are not import-complete in isolation). From cell 7 onward the notebook alternates one short '###' markdown header per one code cell defining a single class - a clean header+code pairing ideal for one-chunk-per-class chunking. Strategy classes inherit from BaseRetrievalStrategy (cell 10), so chunks for strategy classes should carry the base-class context or a reference to it. Pydantic schema classes (relevant_score, SelectedIndices, SubQueries) are defined adjacent to their consuming strategy - keep them in the same chunk. No stored outputs or base64 blobs. Final cell is an analytics tracker image (strip). Markdown/code ratio is roughly 40/60 by cell count; headers are terse but accurate (some typos: 'Adapive', 'reriever').
